commands.review_summary:
The PR adds settings.json-editable BrowserControlPort and BrowserControlToken overrides, wires them into BrowserProxyCapability construction, and adds handler-level unit tests.

Reproducibility: yes. for the review findings: source inspection shows BrowserControlToken is global SettingsData and NodeService still gates browser.proxy registration on sharedGatewayToken. The underlying split/remote gateway failure was not live-reproduced here.
commands.review_followup: - [P1] Needs maintainer decision on the per-gateway credential/storage contract plus contributor-owned real behavior proof; no automation-safe repair lane should run yet.

target.title: browser.proxy: allow overriding the browser-control endpoint port and auth token for split/remote gateways
target.body:
`BrowserProxyCapability` resolves the browser-control host as `gatewayPort + 2` on `127.0.0.1` and presents the node's shared gateway token. That is correct when the gateway is co-located on the same Windows box. It breaks for a **split/remote gateway** — e.g. the gateway running in WSL2 with the Companion on the Windows host reaching it over a forwarded port: the browser-control host can be on a different local port, and it authenticates against the gateway's `gateway.auth.token`, not the node's per-device token. Result: every `browser.proxy` request fails (unreachable, or 401 — the capability even retries Basic/password and still can't match).

This adds two optional overrides so the Companion can be pointed at the correct browser-control endpoint and present the token that host accepts. **Defaults are unchanged** — when both are unset the behaviour is exactly today's (`gatewayPort + 2`, shared token), so co-located installs do not move.

## Changes
- `OpenClaw.Shared/SettingsData.cs` — add `BrowserControlPort` (int?) and `BrowserControlToken` (string?). Null/blank = derive as today.
- `OpenClaw.Tray.WinUI/Services/SettingsManager.cs` — forwarders for the two settings.
- `OpenClaw.Shared/Capabilities/BrowserProxyCapability.cs` — ctor gains `controlPortOverride` + `browserControlToken`; `TryResolveControlEndpoint` honours an explicit port (TCP-range validated) else `gatewayPort + 2`; effective bearer = `browserControlToken` when set, else the shared gateway token.
- `OpenClaw.Tray.WinUI/Services/NodeService.cs` — pass the two settings into the capability ctor.
- `tests/OpenClaw.Shared.Tests/CapabilityTests.cs` — +3 `[Fact]`: port override targets the configured port; token override sends the browser-control token (not the node token); out-of-range port errors cleanly.

## Scope boundary
- Settings are settings.json-editable; **no Settings UI added** (advanced/optional override).
- **No gateway-side change** — Companion-only.
- Overrides apply at node (re)registration; not hot-reloaded mid-session.

## Tested
- `dotnet build src/OpenClaw.Tray.WinUI -c Debug -r win-x64` -> Build succeeded.
- `dotnet test tests/OpenClaw.Shared.Tests` -> 2268 passed, 0 failed, 29 skipped (incl. the 3 new).
- End-to-end in a clean Windows 11 Hyper-V VM (.NET 10): build + full `Shared.Tests` green inside the guest (2268/0/29, rc=0).

## Not tested
- No Settings UI, so no UI test.
- No automated CI test against a live remote/split gateway — the unit tests cover endpoint + token resolution; the real-world repro that motivated this was a WSL2 <-> Windows packet capture.
- Hot-reload on settings change not wired.

## Risk / mitigation
- *Wrong token sent if misconfigured* -> null/blank falls back to today's shared-token path; only an explicit value overrides.
- *Token stored plaintext in settings.json* -> consistent with the existing gateway-token storage posture; documented in the property summary.
- *Port override out of range* -> validated, returns a clear error instead of a bad URI.
